# Should I Buy a Diesel or Petrol Cheap Car?

So, you're on the hunt for a bargain motor here in the UK. Fantastic! But once you've set your budget, a common question pops up: **should I buy a diesel or petrol cheap car**? It's a proper head-scratcher, isn't it? Both have their pros and cons, especially when you're looking at the more affordable end of the market.

At BuyAndScrap.com, we're here to help you navigate this decision with some plain English, honest advice. Let's dig into the details to help you figure out which fuel type will suit your wallet and driving style best.

## The Great Debate: Diesel vs. Petrol for Your Wallet

When you're keeping costs down, every penny counts. Here’s how the fuel types stack up financially.

### Initial Purchase Price

Generally speaking, when you're looking at cheap cars, petrol models tend to have a lower upfront purchase price. Diesels, especially newer, more efficient ones, often command a slightly higher premium. However, for older, budget-friendly cars, this difference might be less pronounced.

### Fuel Economy

This is where diesel traditionally shines. If you do a lot of motorway miles or longer journeys, a diesel engine will almost certainly give you better miles per gallon (MPG). While diesel fuel itself can sometimes be slightly more expensive at the pump, the improved efficiency often balances this out, or even puts you ahead. Petrol cars, especially older ones, can be quite thirsty.

### Road Tax (VED)

The rules around Vehicle Excise Duty (VED), or road tax as we commonly know it, have changed over the years. For older cheap cars, road tax is usually based on CO2 emissions. It's always worth checking the specific car's VED band before buying, as some older models might be surprisingly expensive, regardless of fuel type. Newer cars (post-2017) have a flatter rate.

### Insurance Costs

For a cheap car, the insurance cost difference between diesel and petrol is often negligible. Factors like engine size, your age, driving history, and the car's security rating will typically have a much bigger impact than the fuel type alone.

### Maintenance and Repairs

This is a big one for cheap cars. While modern diesels are marvels of engineering, their complexity can lead to costly repairs if things go wrong. Components like Diesel Particulate Filters (DPFs), turbochargers, and fuel injectors can be incredibly expensive to replace. Petrol engines, particularly simpler, naturally aspirated ones often found in cheaper cars, can be less prone to these high-cost failures and generally cheaper to maintain. If you're buying an older diesel, ensure it has a full-service history, particularly regarding DPF maintenance.

## Driving Style & Usage: What's Your Commute Like?

Your daily driving habits are crucial in deciding **should I buy a diesel or petrol cheap car**.

### Short Journeys & City Driving

If most of your driving involves short hops to the shops, school runs, or stop-start city traffic, a petrol car is almost always the better choice. Diesel engines, particularly those with DPFs, need to get up to temperature and have a decent run to regenerate the filter. Frequent short journeys can clog the DPF, leading to costly repair bills and breakdowns.

### Long Journeys & Motorway Driving

This is diesel's territory. If you regularly commute long distances or often embark on motorway trips, a diesel car will not only be more fuel-efficient but also generally more relaxed to drive, thanks to their strong low-end torque.

### Performance

For a cheap car, blistering performance isn't usually the top priority. Diesels tend to offer more torque, giving you that 'shove in the back' feeling for easier overtaking, especially on motorways. Petrol cars typically rev higher and can feel a bit livelier in lower gears.

## Environmental Considerations (and UK Regulations)

The environmental impact and associated regulations are now a huge factor for UK car buyers.

### Emissions & Clean Air Zones (CAZ/ULEZ)

This is perhaps the biggest consideration for many UK drivers, especially if you live in or regularly travel into major cities. Many older diesel cars (typically pre-Euro 6 standard, mandatory for new cars from 2015) are subject to charges in Clean Air Zones (CAZ) and London's Ultra Low Emission Zone (ULEZ). Failing to comply can mean daily charges, quickly making your 'cheap car' very expensive. Always check if a car is ULEZ/CAZ compliant using the government's checker or TfL's tool before you buy.

Newer Euro 6 diesels are much cleaner and generally exempt from these charges, but they might be harder to find in the 'cheap car' category. Petrol cars generally fare better, with most petrol cars registered after 2005 (Euro 4) being compliant.

## So, Should I Buy a Diesel or Petrol Cheap Car?

Ultimately, there's no single right answer to **should I buy a diesel or petrol cheap car**. It boils down to your personal circumstances and priorities:

*   **Choose Petrol if:** You mostly do short journeys, drive in cities (especially ULEZ/CAZ areas), prefer lower upfront costs, and want potentially cheaper maintenance on an older vehicle.
*   **Choose Diesel if:** You regularly do long journeys, spend a lot of time on motorways, prioritise fuel economy over everything else, and can find a compliant Euro 6 model within your budget that has a solid service history.

Regardless of your fuel choice, when buying a cheap used car, always carry out a thorough inspection, check the service history, MOT certificates, and ideally get a vehicle history check. These steps are crucial for ensuring your bargain doesn't turn into a money pit! You can find more helpful advice in our [used car buying checklist](/buying-a-used-car-checklist). For information on low emission zones, check out our guide to [understanding ULEZ and CAZ](/understanding-ulez-and-caz).

Ready to find your next set of wheels?
